1: Ephesus was the largest city in the Roman province of Asia (modern western Turkey) — a provincial capital, a major Mediterranean port, and home to the Temple of Artemis, one of the seven wonders of the ancient world. Paul's three-year residence there (Acts 19:8-10) made it the base for evangelizing the entire province. The phrase "in Ephesus," however, is absent from several of the earliest manuscripts (P46, Codex Vaticanus, Codex Sinaiticus), which simply read "to the holy ones who are also faithful in Christ Jesus." Many scholars conclude Ephesians was a circular letter sent to multiple churches in the region, with the place name left blank to be filled in by the letter-carrier for each destination.
2: The greeting "grace and peace" (χάρις καὶ εἰρήνη) fuses the standard Greek epistolary greeting (chairein) with the Hebrew shalom (שָׁלוֹם). Paul's adaptation is not merely stylistic — by attributing both to "God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ," he places the two on the same footing as the source of the one unified blessing. The formula appears in every Pauline letter and is now so familiar that its novelty is easily missed: a Jewish blessing and a Greek greeting merged into a new form that signals both communities are addressed.
3: The opening "Blessed be God" (εὐλογητὸς ὁ θεός) is a Jewish berakah (blessing) formula found throughout the OT and Second Temple literature and in synagogue liturgy. The long sentence that follows (Ephesians 1:3–14 in Greek, a single 202-word sentence) is structured like a Jewish prayer of thanksgiving, cataloguing God's acts before making any petition. The phrase "heavenly realms" (ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις) appears five times in Ephesians and nowhere else in Paul — it signals a vertical dimension of reality inhabited by Christ, angelic powers, and now believers. This distinctive spatial frame is central to Ephesians' theology.
4: "Holy and blameless" echoes the cultic language of sacrifice — the animals brought to the altar were required to be tamim (without defect, unblemished). Paul transfers this vocabulary from the sacrificial system to the people of God, signalling that what the cult sought through unblemished animals, God purposes in the community through Christ. "Before the foundation of the world" reflects Jewish cosmological thinking, widespread in Second Temple literature, that divine choices precede creation itself (cf. 1 Enoch 39; 4 Ezra 6:6). Election is not a late divine reaction to human events but a pre-creational decision.
5: Roman adoption (adoptio) was a deliberate legal act that transferred a person fully into a new family, conferring the adopter's name, citizenship, and inheritance rights — sometimes used by wealthy men without biological heirs to secure a successor. Unlike birth sons, adopted sons were chosen, making adoption in Roman experience an expression of intentional, deliberate relationship. Paul's Gentile readers in Roman Asia Minor would have grasped the force of this immediately: they are not accidental members of the divine family but chosen and formally incorporated into it.
6: "The One he loves" (τοῦ ἠγαπημένου) is a Semitic idiom for the uniquely beloved — the same phrase stands behind "my beloved Son" at Jesus's baptism (Mark 1:11) and transfiguration (Mark 9:7). By placing this at the end of the first strophe ("to the praise of his glorious grace, which he has freely given us in the One he loves"), Paul situates all grace within the Father's love for the Son. The grace believers receive is an extension of that prior, eternal love.
7: Redemption (ἀπολύτρωσις) was a commercial and legal term for the release of a slave or prisoner of war through a ransom payment (lytron). Manumission — the formal purchase and liberation of enslaved persons — was a familiar legal reality across the Roman world; temples sometimes served as intermediaries holding the purchase price. Paul's audience would have understood the term concretely, not abstractly. The "forgiveness of trespasses" (παραπτωμάτων) uses the standard Jewish covenant term for acts of faithlessness against God's covenant obligations.
8: The pairing of "wisdom and understanding" (σοφίᾳ καὶ φρονήσει) echoes the Jewish wisdom tradition, where wisdom (חָכְמָה, hokhmah) and understanding (בִּינָה, binah) are characteristic divine attributes extended to those in covenant relationship (Proverbs 3:19-20; Wis Ephesians 8:18). The idea of God "lavishing" these on believers uses abundance language characteristic of Ephesians — God gives beyond measure, in the manner of a great patron with inexhaustible resources.
9: "Mystery" (μυστήριον) in Paul's usage must be distinguished from its use in the Greco-Roman mystery cults (Eleusinian, Dionysian, Mithraic), where secret rites were disclosed only through prolonged initiation, and the content was kept hidden from outsiders on pain of divine punishment. Paul's mystērion follows the Jewish apocalyptic tradition, where raz (mystery) refers to divine secrets disclosed to prophetic seers (Daniel 2:18-19; 1 Enoch Ephesians 103:2). The crucial difference: Paul's mystery is now openly proclaimed — what was hidden is now published to all.
10: "When the times reach their fulfillment" (πλήρωμα τῶν καιρῶν) is eschatological language for the appointed moment when God's plan reaches completion. Jewish eschatology expected "this age" (עוֹלָם הַזֶּה, olam hazeh) to give way to "the age to come" (עוֹלָם הַבָּא, olam haba); Paul presents the Christ-event as the hinge. The verb "to bring unity to all things" (ἀνακεφαλαιώσασθαι) literally means "to sum up under one head" — a rhetorical term for recapitulating an argument's main points. Paul uses it for Christ as the cosmic head who draws all scattered reality under his unifying lordship.
11: "We who were the first to put our hope in Christ" likely refers to Jewish believers who lived within the messianic expectation of the covenant before the Gentiles were included. The sequence — Jews first, then Gentiles (Ephesians 1:11-13) — mirrors Paul's "to the Jew first and also to the Greek" pattern (Romans 1:16). The "predestined according to the plan of him who works out everything in conformity with the purpose of his will" continues the pre-creational election language of Ephesians 1:4, now with the forensic note that God's plan executes according to his own counsel, not external constraint.
12: "For the praise of his glory" closes the first strophe of the berakah (Ephesians 1:6), the second (Ephesians 1:12), and will close the third (Ephesians 1:14). This triple refrain structures the prayer as a three-movement blessing, each movement ending in the same doxological phrase. Repetition of a key phrase at structural intervals is a Hebrew rhetorical device; its presence here reinforces the Jewish liturgical character of the passage.
13: Being "sealed with the promised Holy Spirit" imports the commercial and legal practice of sealing (σφραγίζω). Seals authenticated ownership and guaranteed authenticity — a sealed document could not be opened before the appointed time without destroying evidence of tampering. In Jewish tradition, circumcision was described as the "seal" of the Abrahamic covenant (Romans 4:11). Paul now replaces circumcision with the Spirit as the seal of the new covenant community — a seal accessible to all, male and female, Jew and Gentile. This is among Paul's most pointed implicit arguments against requiring circumcision of Gentile believers.
14: The "deposit guaranteeing our inheritance" (ἀρραβών) is a commercial law term for earnest money — a down payment that legally binds the full transaction to follow. The word is actually a Semitic loanword in Greek, borrowed from Phoenician commercial practice and widely used in Greco-Roman contracts. The concept is precise: the Spirit is not merely a pledge in an informal sense but a legally binding first installment that guarantees the full inheritance will be delivered. The "until the redemption of those who are God's possession" uses the same manumission language as Ephesians 1:7.
15: Paul's thanksgiving and prayer section follows the standard Hellenistic epistolary form: after the opening salutation, the writer gives thanks for the recipients and reports praying for them (cf. Romans 1:8-10; Colossians 1:3-4; Philippians 1:3-5). This was a courtesy convention in ancient letters, but Paul fills it with theological content. "Your faith in the Lord Jesus and your love for all God's people" — hearing reports of a community's character before visiting them was a standard feature of ancient correspondence, where distance made personal knowledge depend on travelers' reports.
16: "Remembering you in my prayers" — Paul participates in the Jewish pattern of fixed prayer times (the tefillah, morning and evening; cf. Acts 3:1; Ephesians 10:3, 9; Daniel 6:10). His intercessory prayer is not spontaneous recollection but structured, regular liturgical memory. The word translated "remembering" (μνείαν ποιούμενος) is a technical phrase for formal memorial — the kind of deliberate recall that shaped Jewish prayer practice.
17: "The Spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of him" — in Jewish thought, the Spirit is the agent of prophetic insight and divine wisdom (Isaiah 11:2; Numbers 11:25; Wis Ephesians 7:7). The goal, "the knowledge of him" (ἐπίγνωσις αὐτοῦ), echoes the covenantal knowledge formula of Jeremiah 31:34: "They will all know me." This is not merely intellectual acquaintance but relational recognition — the goal of covenant life.
18: "The eyes of your heart" — in Hebrew anthropology, the heart (lēb / לֵב) was the seat of understanding, will, and decision, not primarily of emotion as in modern Western usage. Proverbs repeatedly addresses the heart as the center of moral cognition (Proverbs 4:23; Ephesians 23:7). The "enlightening" of the heart's eyes is a prayer for cognitive and volitional transformation — the capacity to perceive the reality of what God has done. This is a Hebraic framing, not a Platonic or Stoic one.
19: "His incomparably great power for us who believe" — Paul begins to pile up power-vocabulary in a sequence of four Greek terms (δύναμις, ἐνέργεια, κράτος, ἰσχύς) that together convey overwhelming force. The dense accumulation echoes Jewish doxological language for divine power (Psalms 29:1; Isaiah 40:26). This power language activates the Exodus background — the "mighty hand and outstretched arm" (יָד חֲזָקָה, yad chazaqah) of God in Exodus is the paradigm for divine rescue.
20: The right hand position was the place of highest honor in ancient Near Eastern and Greco-Roman court protocol — the king's most trusted vizier sat at his right hand (cf. Psalms 110:1). Paul's statement that God "seated him at his right hand in the heavenly realms" presents Christ's exaltation in political terms: he is the supreme royal official of the divine court. The "heavenly realms" again signals the cosmic dimension of this enthronement.
21: "All rule and authority, power and dominion" — these four terms reflect the hierarchy of spiritual beings in Second Temple Jewish cosmology (cf. 1 Enoch Ephesians 61:10; Colossians 1:16; Romans 8:38). Jewish apocalyptic literature populated the cosmos with ranked angelic and demonic powers, each governing specific domains. Paul's point is not to affirm the cosmology in detail but to use its terms as an exhaustive sweep: Christ is above every member of every tier of the cosmic hierarchy, including powers whose names have not yet been named.
22: "Head over everything for the church" fuses two conceptual traditions. In Hellenistic political philosophy, the body-politic had a head (the ruler) and members (citizens); the head-body metaphor was used for the relationship between ruler and state (Philo employs it). In Jewish wisdom tradition, Wisdom was present at creation and pervades all things (Wis Ephesians 7:24). Paul combines these: Christ as head is simultaneously the cosmic ruler and the source of the body's life — not merely governor but constitutive source.
23: "The fullness of him who fills everything in every way" — plēroma (fullness) became a technical term in later Gnostic cosmology, but here it is grounded in the Jewish theology of divine presence. The glory of the Lord "filled" the tabernacle and temple (Exodus 40:34-35; 1 Kings 8:10-11); the eschatological hope was that God's presence would fill all the earth (Numbers 14:21; Habakkuk 2:14). Paul presents Christ's fullness as this universal divine presence, now mediated through the church as his body.
